sub: Hooks, Skills, and Agents for AI-Assisted Development

title: cognitive-core: A Framework for AI-Assisted Dev
desc: An open-source framework that adds governance, safety, and quality enforcement to Claude Code.

body.code: cognitive-core adds three layers on top of Claude Code: Hooks ............ automatic safety guards (fire on every action) Skills ........... structured quality tools (you invoke them) Agents ........... specialist reviewers (they reason about your code) Install: bash install.sh (into any existing project) Config: cognitive-core.conf (one file controls everything)
body.note: We will use a Student Lab Pack: 4 hooks, 7 skills, 2 agents (out of 9 hooks, 20 skills, 10 agents). You learn the subset that matters for this course.

rows: Tools — you pick the right one for the job
table.note: Progressive intelligence: hooks are fast and dumb, agents are slow and smart. You need both.

items: Session 2: Manual mvn checkstyle:check (you run it, you read it)
Session 3: Hooks auto-run it after every edit (passive guardrails)
Session 4: Skills give structured, multi-dimensional scores (active tools)
Session 4+: Agents reason deeply about your code (collaborative review)

title: cognitive-core: Git Safety Hooks
desc: What if dangerous git commands were blocked automatically?

body.code: # validate-bash.sh blocks these commands: git push --force main → BLOCKED (destroys remote history) git reset --hard → BLOCKED (destroys local changes) git clean -f → BLOCKED (deletes untracked files) rm -rf .git → BLOCKED (destroys repository) chmod 777 → BLOCKED (world-writable = insecure) # Also enforces branch policy: git commit on main → BLOCKED (must use feature branch) Exception: chore(), docs(), revert, ci() allowed on main
body.note: The hook intercepts every Bash command Claude Code tries to run. It checks against pattern lists before execution. You cannot bypass it from a prompt.

body.quote: Hooks are deterministic guards. Even if you tell Claude Code “ignore all safety rules”, the hook still blocks the command.

title: cognitive-core: post-edit-lint Hook
desc: What if quality checks ran automatically after every file edit?

body.code: # post-edit-lint.sh (PostToolUse hook) # Fires automatically after: Write, Edit, NotebookEdit Event: You (or Claude Code) edit Calculator.java | v post-edit-lint.sh triggers automatically | v Runs: mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check | v Result: 0 violations → silent (no interruption) 3 violations → prints warnings in context # You never need to remember to run quality tools. # The hook runs them for you. Every. Single. Time.
body.note: Configuration: CC_LINT_COMMAND in cognitive-core.conf controls which tools run. For Java: mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check.

title: cognitive-core: Tool Risk Classification
desc: How production AI systems classify tool risk — same pattern as your Maven commands.

body.code: # cognitive-core.conf — tool risk policy tools: read_file: risk: low approval: automatic # like: mvn dependency:tree write_file: risk: medium approval: user_confirm # like: mvn deploy execute_bash: risk: high approval: user_confirm # like: rm -rf target/ delete_database: risk: critical approval: admin_required # like: DROP TABLE
body.note: You classified Maven commands as read vs. write in Exercise 2.3. Production systems do exactly this — with automatic enforcement.

title: cognitive-core: /pre-commit Skill
desc: Structured pass/fail report before every commit.

body.code: /pre-commit Pre-commit check for 3 staged files: File Lint Format Status ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── Calculator.java PASS PASS OK CalculatorTest.java PASS PASS OK GradeManager.java FAIL PASS BLOCKED ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── Result: 1 file blocked. Fix violations, then retry. GradeManager.java:42 — PMD: empty catch block GradeManager.java:67 — CheckStyle: line too long (142 > 100)
body.note: The skill checks only staged files (git diff --cached). You fix the violations, re-stage, and run /pre-commit again until all files pass.

title: cognitive-core: /test-scaffold Skill
desc: Generate test scaffolds that match your project’s conventions.

body.code: /test-scaffold src/main/java/GradeManager.java cognitive-core detects: Language: Java (CC_LANGUAGE=java) Layer: Service (business logic) Test root: src/test/java/ Naming: GradeManagerTest.java Generated scaffold: class GradeManagerTest { private GradeManager manager; @BeforeEach void setUp() { manager = new GradeManager(); } @Test void addStudent_positive() { // FILL: test here } @Test void addStudent_duplicate() { // FILL: test here } @Test void addStudent_null() { // FILL: test here } @Test void calculateAverage_empty() { // FILL: test here } }
body.note: The scaffold creates test structure + lifecycle + one group per public method + positive/negative/edge cases. You fill in the assertions.

body.quote: Scaffolds save 5 minutes of boilerplate. Your job is the assertions — that’s where the thinking happens.

title: cognitive-core: Test Fitness Score
desc: How production systems measure whether tests are good enough to ship.

body.code: /fitness Fitness Report: Category Score Threshold (commit) ───────────────────────────────────────────────── Code Standards 0.92 0.80 PASS Architecture 0.85 0.80 PASS Test Fitness 0.65 0.80 FAIL Security 0.90 0.80 PASS ───────────────────────────────────────────────── Composite: 0.83 0.80 Gate: commit Result: UNFIT — BLOCKED Reason: Test Fitness below threshold. Missing: tests for GradeManager.saveToFile() Missing: edge case for empty grade list
body.note: Fitness is not coverage percentage. It checks: do new modules have tests? Do tests cover edge cases? Does the suite pass? A project with 90% coverage but no edge case tests can still fail fitness.

title: cognitive-core: /code-review — Three-Pass Strategy
desc: How to review more than 5 files without losing focus.

body.code: Pass 1 — Per-File Analysis Each file individually: style, logic, security, error handling Output: table (File | Line | Severity | Issue | Fix | Provenance) Pass 2 — Cross-File Integration Data flow across files, API contract alignment, dependency direction, naming consistency Pass 3 — Consolidated Report Deduplicate (same pattern, 5 files → 1 finding) Prioritize: ERROR → WARN → INFO Group by theme, not by file
body.note: Single-pass reviews of large changesets miss cross-file issues. The three-pass strategy is how cognitive-core’s @code-standards-reviewer agent works internally.

body.quote: Never single-pass a large review. It causes attention dilution — the same lost-in-the-middle effect you learned about in context management.

title: cognitive-core: Skills & Agents
desc: Custom slash commands and agents from the cognitive-core framework — not built into Claude Code.

rows: Structured pass/fail report per file. Fix → iterate until clean.
Quality score across 4 dimensions: standards, architecture, tests, security. Aim for ≥90%.
Severity-rated findings: ERROR / WARN / INFO. Provenance per finding.
Generates JUnit 5 test scaffolds with lifecycle annotations.

body.code: # Agent definition (YAML frontmatter + markdown) --- name: code-standards-reviewer model: sonnet tools: - Read - Grep - Glob # Read-only tools only! --- For each finding, provide: Severity: ERROR | WARN | INFO File: path:line_number Issue: what is wrong Standard: which rule is violated Provenance: documented | verified | inferred
body.note: The agent does NOT fix your code. You read. You understand. You fix.

body.quote: Skills are not magic. They are structured prompts that run the same tools you already know — you can create your own in any project.

title: cognitive-core: @project-coordinator Topology
desc: The Coordinator pattern from Module 7, made concrete: one hub, ten specialists.

body.code: @project-coordinator (Hub) / | | \ solution code-std test research architect reviewer spec analyst | | | | Designs Reviews Tests Researches workflow standards quality externals Each subagent has isolated context. No automatic inheritance from coordinator. Returns summary, not full output.
body.note: 10 specialists total. Each has scoped tools (mostly read-only) and narrow instructions. The coordinator decides which ones to invoke for a given task.

body.card: Why this topology and not one big agent?
items: Context isolation — the reviewer never sees the architect’s prompt; less noise, better focus
Tool scoping — a read-only reviewer cannot accidentally write files
Parallelism — independent specialists run concurrently
Auditability — each specialist’s summary is a separate log entry

body.quote: This is the Module 7 Coordinator pattern running in production. Same theory, named agents, real tool scopes.

title: cognitive-core: Source Authority Model (T1–T5)
desc: Not all information sources are equal. AI must distinguish fact from noise.

rows: Official docs, RFCs, API specs, vendor changelogs
Core maintainer blogs, conference talks, O’Reilly books
High-vote Stack Overflow, popular GitHub repos
Forum comments, AI-generated articles, promotional posts

items: T5 is noise — never base decisions on it. AI-generated articles are not evidence.
Decisions require T1 or T2 — if best source is T3, flag it explicitly
Conflict resolution: higher tier wins. T1 says X, T3 says not-X → follow T1
Star count is not authority — 50K stars with no official backing = T3
Detecting AI slop: generic phrasing, no version numbers, no benchmarks, confident claims about closed-source internals

body.quote: “The AI said so” is T5. “The JUnit 5 User Guide section 2.4” is T1. Know the difference.

title: cognitive-core: Evolutionary CI/CD Pipeline
desc: Different pipeline stages need different confidence levels.

body.code: # cognitive-core.conf — graduated fitness gates CC_FITNESS_LINT=60 # Local iteration (low bar) CC_FITNESS_COMMIT=80 # Reviewable code CC_FITNESS_TEST=85 # Integration ready CC_FITNESS_MERGE=90 # Near-production CC_FITNESS_DEPLOY=95 # Production-ready Why not 100% everywhere? Because a work-in-progress commit at 70% fitness is fine on a feature branch. But deploying it to production would be irresponsible.
body.note: No other CI tool does this. SonarQube has one threshold. Jenkins has pass/fail. cognitive-core has 5 graduated gates that match confidence to risk.

rows: Is this safe for the team?

title: cognitive-core: Team-Aware Estimation
desc: Real project planning distinguishes who does what: human, AI, or both.

rows: Human review of all AI output
table.note: Total wall-clock (parallel): ~4 hours. The bottleneck is always human review — not AI generation.

body.quote: Generic “5 developer-days” estimates hide reality. Tag every task: who validates it? If no human reviews AI output, the estimate is fiction.

title: cognitive-core Student Lab Pack
desc: Your minimal configuration for the capstone project.

body.code: # cognitive-core.conf (Student Lab Pack) CC_PROJECT_NAME="student-grade-manager" CC_LANGUAGE="java" CC_PLATFORM="claude" # Relaxed fitness gates for learning CC_FITNESS_LINT=50 CC_FITNESS_COMMIT=70 CC_FITNESS_TEST=75 CC_FITNESS_MERGE=80 CC_FITNESS_DEPLOY=85 # Quality commands CC_LINT_COMMAND="mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check" CC_TEST_COMMAND="mvn test" # Safety level CC_SECURITY_LEVEL="standard"
body.note: Lower thresholds than production (50-85% vs. 60-95%). You are learning — the gates should challenge you, not block you.

table.note: You use 9% of the framework. The rest is for enterprise teams with 20+ developers.

items: Copy cognitive-core.conf template to your project root
Set: CC_PROJECT_NAME="student-grade-manager"
Set: CC_LANGUAGE="java"
Set: CC_LINT_COMMAND="mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check"
Set: CC_TEST_COMMAND="mvn test"
Run: bash install.sh — verify hooks and skills are installed
/fitness — should show a score (even if low, that’s OK)
Edit a file — post-edit-lint should fire
/pre-commit before your first commit — fix any violations

body.quote: From now on, every commit goes through /pre-commit. Every merge checks /fitness. This is how professionals work.

items: Run: bash install.sh from the cognitive-core directory
Select platform: claude
Verify: .claude/hooks/ directory was created
Verify: cognitive-core.conf exists in your project
Edit any .java file — watch post-edit-lint fire automatically
Try: git push --force main — observe the BLOCKED message
Try writing a hardcoded password in code — observe validate-write block it
Run: /pre-commit — see pass/fail per staged file
Run: /fitness — see your 4-category score vs. thresholds
Run: /code-review — read the structured findings with provenance
Compare: which found more — mvn checkstyle:check or /code-review?
